summary: DHH argues that Elon Musk's takeover of Twitter has failed to deliver on its promise of principled management, as evidenced by the ElonJet controversy. While acknowledging that the Twitter Files revealed Old Twitter's Trust & Safety team operated with clear political bias, he contends that Musk's Twitter will likely replicate the same problems from the opposite direction. Using an extended Lord of the Rings metaphor, DHH compares Twitter's power over public discourse to the One Ring — a corrupting force no individual can wield responsibly. His conclusion is that the best outcome would be Twitter's destruction entirely.
key_points: Musk's handling of ElonJet showed he cannot resist the corrupting influence of controlling Twitter's platform
The Twitter Files confirmed that Old Twitter's moderation team operated with systematic political bias
New Twitter under Musk will likely replicate the same censorship problems, just from the opposite ideological direction — a difference in degree, not kind
No individual or faction should control a platform with Twitter's unique influence over public discourse
The best outcome of Musk's acquisition is Twitter's eventual destruction, freeing society from its influence
money_quotes: Nobody should have this much power over the town square. No human is strong enough to resist the corruption it brings.
The central characters in the Orwellianly-named Trust & Safety team were drunk on influence, power, and kept ruling from blatant bias, while draping themselves in bullshit experthood and victimology.
It's now clear that we're probably in store for much of the same, just from the other front. Perhaps it'll be less, but now we're arguing a difference in degree, not in kind.
Our best hope is that Twitter by accident, intent, or happenstance is thrown into the fire of Mount Doom.
No one can handle The One Ring. Nobody should.
key_insight: Twitter's power over public discourse is inherently corrupting regardless of who controls it, and the best outcome for society is the platform's destruction rather than a change in management.
